items.q: Does 'nature versus nurture' mean only one of the two shapes behaviour?
items.correct: No; most psychologists now see behaviour as resulting from an interaction of nature and nurture.
items.misconception: Behaviour must be caused by either genes or environment, but never both.
items.random_wrong: Nature refers to outdoor environments and nurture to indoor ones.
items.why: Students treat the debate as either/or rather than recognising interaction between the two.

items.q: What does it mean for research to be 'socially sensitive'?
items.correct: It has potential consequences for the participants or the wider groups they represent.
items.misconception: It simply means the participants were emotional during the study.
items.random_wrong: It means the study used a very small sample.
items.why: Learners read 'sensitive' as emotional rather than as having social implications.

items.q: Is psychology automatically a science because it uses experiments?
items.correct: Not automatically; being scientific also requires objectivity, control and replicable findings, which are debated.
items.misconception: Any use of an experiment makes a subject fully scientific.
items.random_wrong: Psychology can never use any scientific methods.
items.why: Students assume experiments alone settle the 'science' debate, overlooking other criteria.

items.q: The debate about how far genes versus upbringing shape behaviour is the:
items.why: It's Nature-nurture debate. “Free will debate” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Judging another culture by the norms of your own is known as:
items.why: Correct answer: Ethnocentrism. “Determinism” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Research that could have negative consequences for the groups studied is described as:
items.why: It's Socially sensitive. “Nomothetic” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Explaining behaviour by breaking it down into its simplest components is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Reductionism. “Holism” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
